# Data Preparation

## Required data structure

Both `Golden()` and `gwzinbr()` accept a plain `data.frame` (not an `sf` object).
Each row must correspond to one spatial unit (e.g., district, county, grid cell).
The minimum required columns are:

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| Response | integer | Non-negative count |
| Covariates | numeric | Predictor variables |
| Latitude | numeric | Y-coordinate (see below) |
| Longitude | numeric | X-coordinate (see below) |

Geometry is **not** passed to the model — it is only used for distance calculations.
You can join model results back to an `sf` object for mapping (see @sec-visualizacao).

## Coordinate reference systems {#sec-crs}

The `distancekm` argument controls how inter-point distances are computed:

- **`distancekm = FALSE`** (default): distances are computed in the native units
  of your coordinate columns. Use this with **projected coordinates** (e.g., UTM
  in metres), where Euclidean distances are meaningful.
- **`distancekm = TRUE`**: multiplies the computed Euclidean distance by 111
  (approximate km per degree). Use this with **geographic coordinates**
  (decimal degrees, WGS84) only as an approximation — it ignores latitude-dependent
  distortion.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Coordinate system matters for the bandwidth
The bandwidth `h` returned by `Golden()` is in the same units as your distance
computation. If you use `distancekm = TRUE`, `h` will be in kilometres.
If you use UTM coordinates with `distancekm = FALSE`, `h` will be in metres.
Always document which CRS you used.
:::

### Recommended practice

For analyses over a single country or city, project to a local UTM zone and use
`distancekm = FALSE`. This gives accurate Euclidean distances without approximation.

```r
library(sf)

# Convert sf object to projected UTM
dados_sf <- st_transform(dados_sf, crs = 31983)  # SIRGAS 2000 UTM Zone 23S (Brazil)

# Extract coordinates from the geometry centroid
coords <- st_coordinates(st_centroid(dados_sf))

# Add to data frame (drop geometry for modelling)
dados_modelo <- st_drop_geometry(dados_sf)
dados_modelo$long_utm <- coords[, 1]
dados_modelo$lat_utm  <- coords[, 2]
```

## Choosing spatial reference points {#sec-centroids}

GWR assigns weights based on distances between the **reference points** of each
spatial unit. For polygon data (districts, counties), the default choice is the
geometric centroid. However, the geometric centroid can fall in an uninhabited area
(park, water body, industrial zone).

When the analysis involves population-based counts, consider using
**population-weighted centroids** or the location of **maximum population density**
within each polygon. This ensures the distance metric reflects where people actually
live.

## The offset variable

When the response is a count drawn from populations of different sizes, failing to
account for exposure leads to confounded results — larger areas will mechanically
show more cases. The offset corrects for this.

In **gwzinbr**, the offset must be supplied as the **natural log** of the exposure:

```r
dados_modelo$log_pop <- log(dados_modelo$populacao_total)

# In gwzinbr: offset = "log_pop"
```

Common offset choices:

| Situation | Offset |
|-----------|--------|
| Rate relative to population | `log(population)` |
| Indirect standardisation | `log(expected_cases)` |
| Rate per area | `log(area_km2)` |

## Pre-modelling checklist

Before running `Golden()`, verify:

```{r}
#| label: checklist-exemplo
#| eval: false

library(gwzinbr)
library(MASS)
library(spdep)

data(southkorea_covid19)
d <- southkorea_covid19

# 1. Check overdispersion
mod_pois <- glm(n_covid1 ~ diff_sd, data = d, family = poisson(),
                offset = ln_total)
cat("Residual deviance / df:", deviance(mod_pois) / df.residual(mod_pois), "\n")
# >> >> 1 suggests overdispersion

# 2. Fit NB and check excess zeros
mod_nb <- glm.nb(n_covid1 ~ diff_sd + offset(ln_total), data = d)
mu_hat <- predict(mod_nb, type = "response")
theta  <- mod_nb$theta
zeros_esperados  <- sum((theta / (theta + mu_hat))^theta)
zeros_observados <- sum(d$n_covid1 == 0)
cat("Zeros observados:", zeros_observados,
    "| Zeros esperados (NB):", round(zeros_esperados, 1), "\n")

# 3. Fit global NB and check Moran I on residuals
coords <- cbind(d$x, d$y)
knn    <- spdep::knn2nb(spdep::knearneigh(coords, k = 4))
lw     <- spdep::nb2listw(knn, style = "W")
resid_nb <- residuals(mod_nb, type = "deviance")
spdep::moran.mc(resid_nb, listw = lw, nsim = 999)
```
